notes: walked after vanity--contrasted with "walkest after me in the wilderness" (Jer 2:2): then I was their guide in the barren desert; now they take idols as their guides. (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R.

quotes.text: The "fathers" mentioned in this prophecy may have been those of the long, idolatrous reign of Hezekiah's son Manasseh and grandson Amon (ca. 697-640 B.C.; 2 Kings 21; Jeremiah 15:4). Many of the evils Jeremiah condemned and fought against throughout his mission were the religious and social sins made common in that fifty-seven year period. The reforms of King Josiah no doubt had some good effects; but just as good character grows like a tree and evil multiplies like weeds, so did Judah slip back into evil ways after Josiah's death.
quotes.speaker: Ellis Rasmussen
quotes.source: A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament
quotes.attr: Ellis Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, 543

quotes.text: Chittim--Cyprus, colonized by Phœnicians, who built in it the city of Citium, the modern Chitti. Then the term came to be applied to all maritime coasts of the Mediterranean, especially Greece (Nu 24:24; Isa 23:1; Da 11:30).
quotes.speaker: Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET
quotes.attr: Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET

notes: 11-13. Israel's forsaking of the Lord and her following after false gods of other nations are enumerated as separate evils. That Christ is the "fountain of living waters" spoken of and thus the God of Jeremiah's people is clearly shown in this scripture. Christ told the woman of Samaria that he would give her living water that would spring up unto everlasting life (see John 4:10-14; see also D&C 63:23).

quotes.text: In the book of Revelation, Alpha and Omega (Christ) promises to give freely to him who is athirst "the fountain of the water of life" (see Revelation 21:6; see also D&C 10:66). The book of Isaiah refers to the day of restoration when "with joy shall ye draw water out of the wells of salvation" (Isaiah 12:3; see also 55:1). Latter-day Church leaders often use the phrase "broken cisterns that can hold no water" in describing various fruitless practices or concepts. Elder Marion D. Hanks made this application of the expression: The substitutions we fashion to take the place of God in our lives truly hold no water. To the measure we thus refuse the "living water," we miss the joy we could have. (Conference Report, Apr. 1972, p. 127.)
quotes.speaker: Monte S. Nyman
quotes.attr: Monte S. Nyman, The Words of Jeremiah, p.24

notes: "Living water is the words of eternal life, the message of salvation, the truths about God and his

quotes.text: Too many of our Heavenly Father's children spend their precious lives carving out broken cisterns of worldly gain that cannot hold the living water that satisfies fully their natural thirst for everlasting truth.
quotes.speaker: Joseph B. Wirthlin
quotes.attr: Joseph B. Wirthlin, in Conference Report, Apr. 1995, 23

quotes.text: How natural it was, then, for the Lord and his prophets to use the search for water as a pattern for the search for salvation. Just as a man is spared temporally by drinking the life-giving liquids of mortality, so he is saved eternally by downing great draughts of living water. Moses at Meribah smote the rock and a great flood of water gushed forth to give drink to all Israel and their cattle, thus teaching them that if they would look to Jehovah, by whose power the miracle was wrought, they might drink forever from streams of living water. (Num. 20:1-13.) Jeremiah proclaimed that "the Lord" was "the fountain of living waters." (Jer. 17:13.) And the Lord himself, complaining about the rebellious nature of his people, said: "For my people have committed two evils; they have forsaken me the fountain of living waters, and hewed them out cisterns, broken cisterns, that can hold no water." (Jer. 2:13.) After his resurrection this same Jehovah, identifying himself as the Lord Jesus Christ, spoke similarly to Moroni in these words: "Faith, hope and charity bringeth unto me-the fountain of all righteousness." (Ether 12:28.)
quotes.speaker: Bruce R. Mc Conkie
quotes.source: The Promised Messiah: The First Coming of Christ
quotes.attr: Bruce R. Mc Conkie, The Promised Messiah: The First Coming of Christ, p.204

quotes.text: In verse 13 the two evils committed by Judah are told in figurative terms: (1) They have forsaken the fountain (Jehovah) of living water (life), and (2) they have hewn out broken cisterns (gods) which can hold no water (life). Then the image is changed, and the Lord states that Israel had partaken of the waters of "Sihor" (the Nile) and of "the river" (v. 18) (the Euphrates). In other words, they drank the spiritual waters of Egypt and Babylon and were filled with the lifeless water of idolatry.
quotes.attr: Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 236

quotes.text: The "water out of the wells of salvation" is a symbol also used in Jeremiah 2:13 and John 4:7-14. It is life-giving "water" of new birth unto salvation and atonement.
quotes.speaker: Ellis T. Rasmussen
quotes.source: A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament
quotes.attr: Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, p.513

quotes.text: Noph . . . Tahapanes--Memphis, capital of Lower Egypt, on the west bank of the Nile, near the pyramids of Gizeh, opposite the site of modern Cairo. Daphne, on the Tanitic branch of the Nile, near Pelusium, on the frontier of Egypt towards Palestine. Isa 30:4 contracts it, Hanes. These two cities, one the capital, the other that with which the Jews came most in contact, stand for the whole of Egypt. Tahapanes takes its name from a goddess, Tphnet [CHAMPOLLION]. Memphis is from Man-nofri, "the abode of good men"; written in Hebrew, Moph (Ho 9:6), or Noph. The reference is to the coming invasion of Judah by Pharaoh-necho of Egypt, on his return from the Euphrates, when he deposed Jehoahaz and levied a heavy tribute on the land (2Ki 23:33-35). Josiah's death in battle with the same Pharaoh is probably included (2Ki 23:29, 30).
quotes.speaker: Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET
quotes.attr: Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET

quotes.text: Sihor--that is, the black river, in Greek, Melas ("black"), the Nile: so called from the black deposit or soil it leaves after the inundation (Isa 23:3). The Septuagint identifies it with Gihon, one of the rivers of Paradise.
quotes.speaker: Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET
quotes.attr: Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET

quotes.text: Indeed, moral standards must be maintained. In large measure, those who are disobedient punish themselves. As the Lord said through Jeremiah: "Thine own wickedness shall correct thee, and thy backslidings shall reprove thee." Those entrusted with judicial responsibility in the kingdom of God must see that the Church remains clean so that the living waters of life flow unimpeded.
quotes.speaker: James E. Faust
quotes.attr: James E. Faust, in Conference Report, Oct. 1997, 77

quotes.text: This ancient prophet points out plainly that in disobedience the children of men always find a penalty involved, just as much so as there is a reward for rendering obedience to the Lord.
quotes.speaker: Joseph L. Wirthlin
quotes.attr: Joseph L. Wirthlin, Conference Report, Apr. 1941, pp. 62-63

notes: 20-30. The second evil spoken of in verse 13, that of following after the false gods of other nations, is further described in these verses. Jehovah reminds Israel of times past when he delivered her out of bondage and she then covenanted to keep his commandments; yet on many subsequent occasions, Israel wandered astray and played the harlot by having an affair with Baal or Baalim, the false gods of the surrounding nations. Baal was worshiped in high places among groves of trees-and this included temple prostitution. Therefore, the description of Israel as a harlot carries a double meaning: a literal act of immorality and a figurative representation of Israel's forsaking her husband (Jehovah) to have an affair with another god. The Lord next declares that Israel had been planted by him as a noble vine, wholly a good seed, and asks why she has become a degenerate and strange vine to him. He further describes her apostasy as an internal or spiritual one. and her uncleanliness as that which cannot be washed away with soap or cleansing agents. Israel's or Judah's pretended impotence is refuted by the Lord. and he compares his wayward people to two familiar animals. The first is a swift dromedary which has gone against her training. A dromedary is a one-humped camel especially bred and trained for riding. Israel is therefore accused of knowingly forsaking the commandments of God. Secondly, the Lord compares her to a wild ass who has learned the way of the wilderness and is free to follow the desires of her heart. He then asks, "In her occasion who can turn her away?" The question seems to be, "Who can take away her freedom?" The King James Version of the Bible marginal note reads "or, reverse it?" The Joseph Smith Translation of the Bible (often referred to as the "Inspired Version") renders the answer, "All they that seek her will weary themselves; in her month they shall not find her" (verse 24; italics added). If she follows her natural instincts for freedom, she will not be caught. Other commentators, following the corrupted text, suggest that "on her occasion" and "in her month" refer to the male animals seeking her out in the time of her periodic breeding seasons, during which time she will be caught. The Joseph Smith Translation of the Bible (often referred to as the "Inspired Version") fits the context of the following verses better. The Lord admonishes the wild ass (Judah) to "withhold thy foot from being unshod, and

quotes.text: Israel Symbolized by Vineyard and Vines.-The aptness of our Lord's representation of Israel as a vineyard could not have escaped the perception of the Jews, to whom Old Testament similes of analogous form were familiar figures. Notable among others is the striking picture presented by Isaiah (5:1-7), in which the well provided vineyard is shown as producing wild grapes only, for which grievous disappointment of his expectations the owner determined to break down the wall, remove the hedge, and leave the vineyard to its fate of abandonment. The explication of the parable voiced by Isaiah is thus given: "For the vineyard of the Lord of hosts is the house of Israel, and the men of Judah his pleasant plant: and he looked for judgment, but behold oppression; for righteousness, but behold a cry." The worthlessness of a vine save only for its fruit was set forth by the Lord through His prophet Ezekiel (15:2-5); and truly it is so, that the wood of the grape plant is fit for nothing but burning; the whole vine as wood is inferior to a branch from a forest tree (verse 3). And Israel is represented as such a vine, precious if but fruitful, otherwise nothing but fuel and that of poor quality. The psalmist sang of the vine that Jehovah had brought out of Egypt and which, planted with care and hedged about, had flourished even with goodly boughs; but the favor of the Lord had been turned from the vine, and it had been left desolate (Ps. 80:8-16). For further allusions see Isa. 27:2-6; Jer. 2:21; Ezek. 19:10-14; Hosea 10:1.
quotes.speaker: James E. Talmage
quotes.source: Jesus the Christ: A Study of the Messiah and His Mission According to Holy Scriptures Both Ancient and Modern
quotes.attr: James E. Talmage, Jesus the Christ: A Study of the Messiah and His Mission According to Holy Scriptures Both Ancient and Modern, p.502

notes: nitre - carbonate of soda from the root nathar "to start up, tremble, shake, spring up"

quotes.text: Withhold, &c.--that is, abstain from incontinence; figuratively for idolatry [HOUBIGANT].

quotes.text: Why trimmest--MAURER translates, "How skilfully thou dost prepare thy way," &c. But see 2Ki 9:30. "Trimmest" best suits the image of one decking herself as a harlot.
quotes.speaker: Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET
quotes.attr: Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET

quotes.text: Also--not only art thou polluted with idolatry, but also with the guilt of shedding innocent blood [MAURER]. ROSENMULLER not so well translates, "even in thy skirts," &c.; that is, there is no part of thee (not even thy skirts) that is not stained with innocent blood (Jer 19:4; 2Ki 21:16; Ps 106:38). See as to innocent blood shed, not as here in honor of idols, but of prophets for having reproved them (Jer 2:30; Jer 26:20-23).
quotes.speaker: Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET
quotes.attr: Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET
